row_range.sms_secondary_section: ROW-7763 to ROW-7774 (secondary SMS table, Feb 24 – Mar 3)

primary_flags.verbatim: Her being nervous to talk to you isn't new. We've had conversations about that before where I told you she wanted me to call and talk to you about something because she was afraid to talk to you [ROW-9131] / She asked me to talk to you. That's what you aren't understanding. [ROW-9141] / She didn't feel comfortable talking to you about it. She asked me to. [ROW-9142] / That's why I'm involved [ROW-9143]
primary_flags.context: Part of the Feb 15 'chair dispute' — Melissa contacted Eli claiming Olivia has to sit on the floor to eat at his house and has no proper chair (ROW-9100-9115). Eli disputed (ROW-9106, 9108, 9121: 'There is a chair she can use / Nobody is making her sit on the floor / Olivia has a chair to sit in'). Melissa escalated the dispute by asserting Olivia is too afraid/nervous to raise concerns with Eli directly. Eli explicitly rejected mediated communication (ROW-9133: 'I'll check in with her myself'; ROW-9137: 'I don't need a mediated conversation. Olivia and I have always been able to talk'; ROW-9144: 'I'll check in with Olivia myself. If she truly feels uncomfortable talking to me, I want to address that directly with her so we can work through it together.'). Also appears in MMS: ROW-12958, ROW-12968-12970. ROW-9134 (Melissa): 'With all 3 in the conversation she would feel more comfortable voicing her concerns' — explicitly advocates for Melissa-mediated three-way conversations for Olivia-Eli communication.
primary_flags.analytical_note: Signal 48 extends the required-intermediary framework from scheduling/access domains into affective and grievance communication. Previously documented intermediary pattern: Melissa gates Eli's direct communication with Olivia about scheduling and plans. Here Melissa asserts permanent intermediary necessity for any substantive Olivia-Eli communication about feelings or concerns: Olivia is 'nervous,' has always been 'afraid' to talk to Eli directly, and asks Melissa to relay her concerns to Eli. This structural claim — if accepted — would make Melissa the permanent intermediary for the child's emotional communications with the other parent, not merely for scheduling. Eli actively resists this extension (ROW-9133, 9137, 9144), which is the first explicit on-record rejection of the intermediary claim. The resistance itself is analytically significant: contrast with Eli's full internalization of the scheduling intermediary norm (ROW-7206, ROW-12439). Eli appears to recognize the stakes of the affective-communication extension even as he has fully accepted the scheduling extension.

peripheral_flags.verbatim: You are ruining her self esteem and self worth [ROW-9116] / She goes to your house and doesn't have a seat at the table, you compare her to Milo, the new baby has taken over her room. She sees you being a dad to Milo and Alice but she gets the bare minimum fro[m you] [ROW-9117] / I didn't need to ask you about this because I believe Olivia. She had lots of details that she wouldn't just be making up [ROW-9118] / I want her to as well. I also want you to not disregard her feelings. You really need to look at how the things you do make her feel even if they seem trivial to you [ROW-9151]
peripheral_flags.note: RF-04 Sev 1 parental fitness attack embedded in chair dispute. Key elements: (1) self-esteem/self-worth destruction claim ('ruining her self esteem'); (2) systemic neglect narrative — no seat at table, displaced by new baby, treated as second-tier compared to Milo (stepson) and Alice (newborn); (3) unilateral belief of Olivia over Eli ('I didn't need to ask you about this because I believe Olivia'); (4) sustained messaging across multiple texts through ROW-9151. RF-09 intersection: ROW-9118 dismisses Eli's contradicting account without investigation ('I believe Olivia. She had lots of details'). Also appears in MMS as ROW-12943-12945, ROW-12978. Milo/Alice comparison is new element — previously RF-04 attacks targeted Eli's parental fitness generally; here the comparison to the new family unit (Abby's son Milo, newborn Alice) is explicit.

RF-04 Sev 1 quick shot. Eli (ROW-9278): 'Idk. I had made plans after.' — he had already taken Olivia to her friend's birthday party at Rule 3 and had made post-party plans; Melissa is now asking him to also take Olivia to an impromptu same-day second party (3-5 PM Get Air). Melissa frames Eli's hesitation about a last-minute second event as choosing plans over his daughter. Context: Eli had proactively arranged his day to be available for the originally planned party; the second event was not pre-planned. Also appears as ROW-13127 in MMS dataset. Standalone Sev 1; no new pattern element.

cooperative_data_points.description: Eli resolves chair dispute proactively — 'FYI, I took what you said into account, and we all ate at the table so that there's no favoritism and everyone has a seat.' Melissa acknowledges: 'I'm glad you listened and took her feelings into consideration.' Collaborative correction and positive acknowledgment.

Melissa proactively informs Eli that Olivia has no school Friday and that Olivia would rather go to Eli's house than Becky's — preemptive access offer relaying Olivia's preference toward Eli. Eli unable to accommodate but Melissa made the offer.

Melissa actively coordinates double birthday party access — gives Eli permission to take Olivia to first party, then spontaneously invites him to take her to a same-day second party so Olivia doesn't miss out. Eli attends both events.

Melissa's medical emergency (likely kidney stone, blood in urine). Eli picks up Olivia from social activity, takes her to dinner, coordinates drop-off and prescription logistics while Melissa is at urgent care. High-quality cooperative emergency response.

near_miss_log.consideration: ARC-RF02-002 near-miss. Eli (ROW-9182): 'She want .e to pick her up tomorrow after work?' Melissa (ROW-9183): 'She said sure.' Relay mechanism present but this is access facilitation, not restriction. Eli is also still operating under the required-intermediary norm (asking Melissa rather than Olivia directly). Not flagged: consistent with baseline relay mechanism already well-documented in arc; facilitative context.

ARC-RF02-002 near-miss. Melissa relays Olivia's emotional distress (snail death) and desire to call Eli: 'One of her snails died / She wants to talk to you' and 'She still wants you / to call.' Contact-facilitation relay — Melissa is enabling access, not restricting it. The relay mechanism structure is the same (Olivia's desire mediated through Melissa) but the direction is access-enabling. Not flagged as primary.

ARC-RF02-001 near-miss. Eli (ROW-9231): 'Still going to voicemail.' Melissa (ROW-9232): 'It's dead.' Olivia's phone was dead; Melissa relays context (ROW-9233: homework, 15-minute window offered, Olivia's morning-call preference relayed). Dead phone provides natural explanation for voicemail; no DND or blocking mechanism. Not flagged.

arc_summary.ARC-RF02-001: no new primary signals; signal_count remains 26; Feb 24 voicemail (dead phone) near-miss — natural explanation
arc_summary.ARC-RF02-002: signal_count now 48; one signal this batch — ROW-9131/9141-9143 (required-intermediary extension into affective communication domain, Feb 15, signal 48); analytically significant: Melissa asserts permanent intermediary necessity for Olivia's grievance/concern communications with Eli, citing prior history of similar mediations; Eli explicitly rejects extension (ROW-9133, 9137, 9144) — first on-record rejection of intermediary claim, contrasting with full internalization of scheduling intermediary norm

batch_notes: Quietest RF-02 batch since B-011. One primary flag (ARC-RF02-002 signal 48) from the Feb 15 chair dispute — analytically significant for extending the required-intermediary framework into affective communication but lower structural weight than the scheduling-domain signals. The Feb 15 spike (164 messages) is dominated by co-parenting dispute about a chair, which resolves cooperatively (ROW-9191-9194). Highest cooperative facilitation density in recent batches: four distinct cooperative data points including two proactive access offers (Feb 24 school-free Friday, Mar 1 birthday parties), Eli's responsive conflict resolution, and joint medical emergency response (Mar 3). The RF-04 content (Feb 15 Milo/Alice comparison, Mar 1 'Plans more important than Olivia') is Sev 1 and notable primarily for the Milo/Alice comparison — new element in the RF-04 pattern that positions Eli as a more invested parent to his other children than to Olivia. Medical context: Melissa likely has a kidney stone (Mar 3 — blood in urine, urgent care, CT scan needed).
